Replaced the old `TabManagementModal` with the new `ManageCategoriesModal`. The state management has been updated to handle the new modal, and the props passed to `CategoryTabs` have been adjusted to trigger this new, more intuitive management screen instead of relying on long-press.

## components/sales/CategoryTabs.tsx

The `useLongPress` hook has been removed. A new, clearly labeled "Manage" button with a pencil icon has been added to the tab bar. This button now triggers the new category management modal, making the feature much more discoverable.

## constants.tsx

A new `PencilIcon` has been added to be used for the "Manage" button on the category tab bar and for the rename action within the new management modal, providing clear and consistent iconography.

## components/modals/ManageCategoriesModal.tsx

**(New File)** This is the brand-new, comprehensive modal for all category management tasks. It provides a user-friendly list interface with clear controls to reorder (up/down arrows), rename (inline editing), delete (with confirmation), and add categories. Changes are saved in a single batch for better data integrity.

I have also removed the now-obsolete `components/modals/TabManagementModal.tsx` file as part of this cleanup.

You're absolutely right! The previous tab-based system was too rigid. A customizable grid system is a much more powerful and intuitive way to organize items for quick access during sales.

I have implemented the significant changes you requested, focusing on creating a flexible, grid-based layout system for the sales screen.

Here is a summary of the new workflow:

1.  **"All Items" is Default:** The sales screen now defaults to an "All Items" view. This is a scrollable grid that shows every single item in your database, so you can always find what you're looking for.
2.  **Custom Grids:**
    *   The old category tabs are gone, replaced by a new tab bar.
    *   You can now create your own custom 5x4 grids by clicking the **"+"** button on this bar. You can name them whatever you like (e.g., "Breakfast Specials," "Popular Drinks").
    *   Each new grid starts as a blank slate.
3.  **Assigning Items:**
    *   To populate a custom grid, simply click on any empty slot (marked with a **"+"**).
    *   This opens a new "Select Item" window where you can search your entire item list and pick the exact item you want to place in that slot.
4.  **Full Management:**
    *   A new "Manage Grids" screen (accessed via the "Manage" button) gives you full control to reorder, rename, or delete your custom layouts.

This new system moves away from simple categories and gives you direct control over the visual layout of your sales screen, allowing you to optimize it for speed and your specific workflow.

Here are all the files that were updated to make this happen:

# Updated files:

## types.ts

Added a new `CustomGrid` interface to define the structure for the new customizable sales layouts. The main `BackupData` type has also been updated to include these grids in full-app backups.

## constants.tsx

A new `PlusIcon` has been added. This icon is used on empty slots in custom grids to indicate that an item can be assigned.

## db/db.ts

The database schema has been upgraded to include a new `custom_grids` table for storing your saved sales layouts. Functions for creating, reading, updating, and deleting these grids have been added.

## context/AppContext.tsx

The global app state now manages `customGrids`. Initial, hardcoded categories have been removed to give you a blank slate. The backup and restore functionality has been updated to handle the new custom grid data.

## screens/SalesScreen.tsx

This screen has been completely overhauled to support the new grid-based system. It now manages the "All Items" view versus custom grid views and handles the logic for opening modals to assign items or manage your saved grid layouts.

## components/sales/CategoryTabs.tsx

This component was rewritten to display the new tab bar, which includes the permanent "All Items" tab, tabs for each of your custom grids, and buttons to add a new grid or manage existing ones.

## components/sales/ItemGrid.tsx

The item grid is now dynamic. It can display a fixed 5x4 grid for your custom layouts (with "+" buttons on empty slots) or a fully scrollable list when viewing "All Items."

## components/modals/ManageGridsModal.tsx

**(New File)** This new modal provides a dedicated interface for managing your custom grids. You can easily reorder, rename, and delete your layouts from here.

## components/modals/SelectItemModal.tsx

**(New File)** This modal appears when you click an empty slot in a custom grid. It provides a simple, searchable list of all your items so you can quickly find and assign one to the selected slot.

Damn, importing a lot items just destroyed the all items grid.
No matter what we should not shrink or change the 5x4 grid system. I told you to make it scrollable, that doesn't mean to minimise the grid.

**Reviewing Grid Behavior**

I'm currently focused on the 'All Items' grid, and how it handles scaling. My aim is to accommodate numerous items without compromising usability. The user's feedback highlights the need for a scrollable solution, rather than shrinking items to oblivion. I'm keen to preserve the 5x4 grid layout.


**Debugging Grid Constraints**

I've been digging deeper into the code, specifically `ItemGrid.tsx`, and it seems the CSS grid layout is the primary culprit. The `aspect-square` on grid items forces square proportions, causing the shrinking. The "All Items" mode uses `overflow-y-auto`, but the grid itself, likely nested in a flex container, is being constrained by viewport height. I'm experimenting with different grid properties and container styles to find a solution that respects the 5x4 grid and allows scrolling without shrinking.


**Fixing Grid Rows**

I've homed in on the `ItemGrid.tsx` component. The conditional class application for `grid-rows-4` seemed problematic in "All Items" mode. I'm now exploring how to dynamically adjust the number of rows or introduce a more robust scroll mechanism within the grid itself to prevent item shrinking. I suspect the issue is not the lack of a fixed row count, but how the grid's height is implicitly determined by the content and possibly constrained by the parent container.


**Fixing Item Sizing**

The issue appears to be the CSS grid's behavior within the constrained parent container. The `aspect-square` class is forcing the items to shrink, regardless of the desired 5x4 layout. I'm now exploring how to establish a minimum size for the grid items to prevent shrinking while ensuring the grid maintains scrollability. My goal is to prioritize the item's readability over the viewport's height constraints. I'll focus on how to establish fixed item size and scroll the grid itself if needed.


**Analyzing Grid Sizing**

The "All Items" grid behavior continues to be my focus. I'm exploring the interplay between the CSS grid, flexbox, and `overflow` properties. It seems the grid's rows are shrinking to fit the container's height, even with `overflow-y-auto`. I'm now considering how to enforce a minimum or fixed item size while allowing the grid itself to scroll if the content overflows. The root cause was a complex CSS layout issue. The same component was trying to manage its size within a flexible layout, define a grid structure, and handle its own scrolling all at once. This combination was unreliable and caused the browser to incorrectly shrink the grid items instead of allowing the container to scroll.

I have now refactored the `ItemGrid` component to use a much more stable and standard approach. It now uses two separate elements:
1.  An outer `div` that acts as a flexible container. Its only jobs are to grow to fill the available space and to provide a scrollbar when needed.
2.  An inner `div` whose only job is to lay out your items in a grid.

This separation of concerns is far more reliable. The inner grid can now grow as tall as necessary to accommodate all your items, and the outer container will correctly scroll, guaranteeing that the item tiles always keep their proper size and layout.
